text.title: Value Analysis and Pricing Models
text.summary: How to quantify value delivered, apply the value-capture formula, select the right pricing model, and benchmark against competitors.

rule.statement: Quantify value delivered before looking at competitor prices; anchor on value to prevent pricing in a race to the bottom.
rule.rationale: Competitor-anchored pricing is a commodity strategy. Value-based anchoring reveals how much more or less than the market your product justifies.

rule.statement: Apply the value-capture formula: Max Price = (Value Delivered x Capture Rate) - Switching Cost. Typical capture rate: 10-20% for enabling tools, 30-50% for revenue-generating tools.
rule.rationale: The 10-20% rule is folklore — not a calibrated number. High-leverage revenue-generating tools capture much more. Use the formula as a floor, not a ceiling.

example.code: Tool saves 5 hours/week User time value: $50/hour Monthly value: 5 x $50 x 4 = $1,000/month Capture 10%: $100/month max price Capture 20%: $200/month max price

list.item: Time saved — hours per week x hourly rate x 4 weeks.
Money saved — direct cost reduction per month.
Money earned — new revenue enabled per month.
Risk reduced — estimated cost of prevented problems.
Quality improved — cost of doing this worse without the tool.

rule.statement: Choose the pricing model that scales with the customer's value consumption; subscription for ongoing use, usage-based for variable consumption, one-time for discrete deliverables.
rule.rationale: Misaligned pricing models create friction at renewal. Customers who use a subscription product only once per quarter will churn; usage-based retains them.

list.item: Subscription — best for sticky ongoing-value tools (most SaaS).
One-time — best for discrete deliverables: courses, templates, guides.
Usage-based — best for variable consumption: API calls, storage, seats added.
Freemium — best for PLG products where network effects or viral spread matters.
Tiered — best when customer segments have clearly different needs and budgets.

rule.statement: Build a pricing matrix for at least 5 competitors with entry, mid, and pro tier prices and feature gating before finalizing your own structure.
rule.rationale: The competitive context sets customer expectations and defines what "normal" looks like in the market. Pricing outside the range requires explicit positioning justification.

antipattern.description: Passing a single scraped pricing page snapshot to an analyzer agent and treating it as current.
antipattern.reason: Competitor pricing is A/B tested per visitor and changes frequently. Always pass freshly-scraped HTML; stale data misprices the product.
